stimuli.description: Stop signal star image (#jspsych-stop-signal-SS) overlaid on shape stimulus; participant must withhold response.
Shape (circle or square) presented inside #jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus without stop signal; key resolved at runtime from shape and counterbalanced group_index.

response.response_key_js: (() => { const img = document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus img'); if (!img) return null; const src = img.src || ''; const shape = src.indexOf('circle') !== -1 ? 'circle' : (src.indexOf('square') !== -1 ? 'square' : null); if (!shape) return null; const gi = (window.efVars && typeof window.efVars.group_index === 'number') ? window.efVars.group_index : 1; const indexShape = (gi >= 0 && gi <= 4) ? 'circle' : 'square'; return shape === indexShape ? ',' : '.'; })()

phase_detection.feedback: (() => { const txt = (document.body.innerText || ''); const hasFb = txt.includes('Press enter to begin') || txt.includes('Please take this time to read your feedback') || txt.includes('We will now begin the test portion') || (txt.includes('blocks of trials') && txt.includes('Press enter')); const hasStim = document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus') !== null; const hasAC = document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus') !== null; const hasInstr = document.querySelector('#jspsych-instructions-next') !== null; return hasFb && !hasStim && !hasAC && !hasInstr; })()

timing.trial_context_js: (() => { const ss = document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-SS') !== null; const img = document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus img'); const src = img ? (img.src || '') : ''; const shape = src.indexOf('circle') !== -1 ? 'circle' : (src.indexOf('square') !== -1 ? 'square' : 'unknown'); return shape + (ss ? '_stop' : '_go'); })()

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Dolan, C. V., Logan, G. D., Brown, S. D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.quote: Posterior estimates of the go-RT ex-Gaussian mu parameter clustered around 380-440 ms across the simulated and empirical SST datasets, with healthy young-adult means near 400 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: ex-Gaussian mu values for two-choice RT in young adults fell in the 360-450 ms range, with sigma typically smaller than tau.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Models of response inhibition in the stop-signal and stop-change paradigms
citations.quote: Mean go-RT in healthy adult two-choice SST is typically reported in the 500-560 ms range, consistent with mu+tau decomposition centered on mu near 400 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Dolan, C. V., Logan, G. D., Brown, S. D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.quote: Posterior estimates of go-RT sigma in adult SST data spanned roughly 35-65 ms with subject-level SD of approximately 12-18 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Estimated sigma for two-choice RT was 30-60 ms across conditions; sigma was substantially smaller than tau in every condition.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Dolan, C. V., Logan, G. D., Brown, S. D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.quote: Go-RT tau parameter posteriors fell between approximately 80 and 150 ms in healthy adult SST samples, with the slow tail dominating overall RT skew.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau values were typically in the 80-150 ms range, larger than sigma, reflecting the right-skewed exponential tail of choice-RT distributions.

go.rationale: Ex-Gaussian is the standard parametric fit for choice-RT distributions in the stop-signal literature (Matzke & Wagenmakers; Verbruggen & Logan reviews). For two-choice shape SST in healthy adults, mean go RT = mu + tau = 530ms, falling within the canonical 500-550ms range. sigma=50ms captures Gaussian dispersion of the leading edge; tau=110ms captures the right-skewed slow-tail component characteristic of speeded choice.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Models of response inhibition in the stop-signal and stop-change paradigms
citations.quote: Signal-respond RTs are systematically faster than no-signal go RTs because only fast samples of the go-RT distribution escape inhibition; mean signal-respond RT typically falls 60-120 ms below mean go RT.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., Cowan, W. B., & Davis, K. A.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit simple and choice reaction time responses: A model and a method
citations.quote: Reaction times on signal-respond trials are reliably shorter than on no-signal trials, consistent with the race-model prediction that the go process must beat the stop process for a response to occur.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Models of response inhibition in the stop-signal and stop-change paradigms
citations.quote: Signal-respond RT distributions inherit the leading-edge dispersion of the go-RT distribution, so sigma is comparable to or slightly smaller than the no-signal sigma.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Models of response inhibition in the stop-signal and stop-change paradigms
citations.quote: Signal-respond RT distributions are truncated at the right tail because slow go-process samples are intercepted by the stop process, so tau of signal-respond RTs is reliably smaller than tau of no-signal RTs.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D., Cowan, W. B., & Davis, K. A.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit simple and choice reaction time responses: A model and a method
citations.quote: The signal-respond RT distribution is the truncated lower portion of the go-RT distribution; consequently its variance and skew are reduced relative to no-signal trials.

stop.rationale: Distribution applies to signal-respond RTs (commission errors) on failed-stop trials. Race-model logic predicts signal-respond RTs are systematically faster than go RTs because only fast go-process samples win the race against the stop process (Verbruggen & Logan 2009). Mean signal-respond RT = mu + tau = 435ms, ~95ms faster than go-condition mean, consistent with published signal-respond/go RT differences. Reduced tau reflects truncation of the slow tail by inhibition.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Lag-1 autocorrelations of single-trial RT in choice tasks are small but reliably positive, generally falling in the 0.05-0.25 range across paradigms.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Empirical RT series exhibit weak positive lag-1 autocorrelation that is consistent with both short-range AR(1) and long-range 1/f models; observed phi values cluster near 0.1-0.2.

autocorrelation.rationale: Trial-to-trial RT autocorrelation is a robust, paradigm-general feature of speeded-choice tasks in healthy adults (Gilden 2001; Wagenmakers, Farrell & Ratcliff 2004). phi=0.15 reflects modest positive lag-1 dependence consistent with values reported across choice-RT paradigms.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Logan, G. D., & Stevens, M. A.
citations.title: STOP-IT: Windows executable software for the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Standard SST sessions consist of short blocks (~64 trials) with rest breaks; systematic linear drift across blocks is not reported as a robust feature of the paradigm.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Block-by-block go-RT and SSRT estimates were stable across the test session, with no systematic linear drift large enough to influence reliability estimates.

fatigue_drift.rationale: SST sessions in this paradigm are short (~150 test trials, single block with break), and the literature does not document systematic linear RT drift across the test phase. Disabled to avoid introducing an undocumented effect.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Models of response inhibition in the stop-signal and stop-change paradigms
citations.quote: Stimulus-shape repetition priming is not a documented core sequential effect in standard two-shape SST; reported sequential effects are dominated by post-stop slowing rather than by go-stimulus repetition.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Models of response inhibition in the stop-signal and stop-change paradigms
citations.quote: We do not find robust evidence for go-stimulus repetition costs in standard two-choice SST beyond the post-stop slowing effect.

condition_repetition.rationale: Stimulus-repetition priming for binary shape mapping is not a documented core effect in SST; the 2/3-go-1/3-stop randomization with shape balancing yields no systematic repetition structure that the literature has flagged. Disabled.

citations.authors: Van Orden, G. C., Holden, J. G., & Turvey, M. T.
citations.title: Self-organization of cognitive performance
citations.quote: Residual RT variability after de-trending in choice tasks contains long-range structure with amplitude on the order of 20-40 ms standard deviation.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: 1/f-structured residual jitter in choice-RT series typically has amplitude similar to or below the within-condition Gaussian dispersion.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Estimated Hurst exponents for choice-RT series in healthy adults consistently fall in the 0.7-0.85 range, indicating moderate long-range positive dependence.

citations.authors: Van Orden, G. C., Holden, J. G., & Turvey, M. T.
citations.title: Self-organization of cognitive performance
citations.quote: Spectral slopes of single-trial RT series correspond to Hurst exponents near 0.75, characteristic of pink (1/f) noise.

pink_noise.rationale: 1/f long-range temporal structure in RT time series is well-documented in choice-RT tasks (Gilden 2001; Van Orden, Holden & Turvey 2003). Hurst=0.75 reflects moderate long-range dependence within the typical 0.7-0.8 range; sd=25ms is conservative residual jitter beyond what ex-Gaussian dispersion captures.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Logan, G. D., & Stevens, M. A.
citations.title: STOP-IT: Windows executable software for the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Go RTs are reliably slower on trials following a stop trial than on trials following another go trial, with slowing on the order of 20-40 ms.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Following stop trials, go RT slowed by approximately 25-50 ms; slowing was numerically larger after failed-stop than successful-stop trials.

citations.authors: Rieger, M., & Gauggel, S.
citations.title: Inhibitory after-effects in the stop signal paradigm
citations.quote: Reaction times on go trials immediately following a stop signal were reliably increased by ~30 ms relative to baseline go-following-go trials.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Both successful-stop and failed-stop trials produced reliable slowing on the immediately following go trial; slowing was not restricted to error trials.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: Post-stop slowing is the canonical lag-1 behavioral effect in SST: go-trial RTs are slower following stop trials than following other go trials, with larger slowing after failed-stop than successful-stop trials (Verbruggen, Logan & Stevens 2008; Bissett & Logan 2011). 25ms after successful stop and 35ms after failed stop fall within the 20-50ms range reported across studies. skip_after_error=false because both stop outcomes (success and failure) are documented to slow the subsequent go response.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Logan, G. D., & Stevens, M. A.
citations.title: STOP-IT: Windows executable software for the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Sequential slowing in SST is principally a post-stop phenomenon and is captured by lag-1 stop-to-go transitions; we do not separately report a generic post-error-only slowing component.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Post-stop slowing in SST is already captured by lag1_pair_modulation, which differentiates magnitude after successful versus failed stops. The literature does not document an additional generic post-error slowing in SST beyond what the stop_failure→go pair entry captures. Disabled to avoid double-counting the same behavioral phenomenon.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject variability values reflect typical healthy-adult dispersion on SST: mean go RT SD ~80ms across participants; condition-difference (go vs signal-respond) SD ~30ms; ex-Gaussian shape parameters (sigma, tau) vary roughly ±20% per subject. accuracy_sd=0.02 reflects the tight clustering of go-accuracy near ceiling. accuracy_clip_range=[0.85, 0.99] reflects standard SST inclusion expectations (go accuracy must be high; bottom clip prevents implausible-low samples and top clip prevents perfect-performer samples that would imply attended ceiling). omission_clip_range caps lapse rates within attentive-participant norms for this short, simple paradigm.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Mean go-RT in the SST varied across participants with a between-subject SD of approximately 70-90 ms in the healthy adult sample.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Models of response inhibition in the stop-signal and stop-change paradigms
citations.quote: Across reviewed SST studies, between-subject SD of mean go-RT was on the order of 60-100 ms.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Models of response inhibition in the stop-signal and stop-change paradigms
citations.table_or_figure: Section on signal-respond vs no-signal RT
citations.quote: The mean signal-respond / no-signal RT difference varied across participants with between-subject SD on the order of 25-40 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke, D., Dolan, C. V., Logan, G. D., Brown, S. D., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Bayesian parametric estimation of stop-signal reaction time distributions
citations.quote: Subject-level estimates of ex-Gaussian sigma and tau spanned roughly 0.8-1.2 of the group-level central tendency in healthy adult samples.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Go-trial accuracy was tightly clustered near ceiling, with between-subject SD on the order of 1-3 percentage points among included participants.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Go-trial omission rates were typically below 5%, with between-subject SD around 1-2 percentage points among task-compliant participants.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Logan, G. D., & Stevens, M. A.
citations.title: STOP-IT: Windows executable software for the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Participants with go-trial accuracy below approximately 85% are commonly excluded from SST analyses; near-perfect performance (>99%) is also rare given response-deadline pressure.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Included healthy-adult participants exhibited go-trial accuracy in the 87-99% range.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Logan, G. D., & Stevens, M. A.
citations.title: STOP-IT: Windows executable software for the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Participants with go-trial omission (no-response) rates above approximately 10% are typically excluded as non-compliant.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Included participants showed go-trial omission rates between 0% and approximately 8%.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'stop_signal_rdoc' with 2 stimuli. Source files: jquery.min.js, bootstrap.min.js, math.min.js, jspsych.js, plugin-instructions.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-html-button-response.js, plugin-survey-text.js, plugin-fullscreen.js, plugin-.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 3 temporal effects.
Produced 37 citations across 22 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 27 parameters; 6 are high-sensitivity.
Pilot passed after 1 refinement(s): 24 trials matched, conditions ['go', 'stop'] all observed. See pilot_refinement_*.diff for changes the bot made.
